posts.title: Google's 24/7 CFE Commitment Holds: What the GHG Protocol Hourly Matching Debate Means for Indian Data Centre Operators, RE100 Signatories, and BRSR Scope 2 Disclosures
posts.summary: Google's Singapore and Malaysia Managing Director Ben King confirmed at the Ecosperity conference that the company's 2030 24/7 Carbon-Free Energy (CFE) commitment — hourly, local matching of electricity consumption with clean energy supply — remains fully intact despite a $190 billion AI infrastruct

sections.summary: Google's Singapore and Malaysia Managing Director Ben King confirmed at the Ecosperity conference that the company's 2030 24/7 Carbon-Free Energy (CFE) commitment — hourly, local matching of electricity consumption with clean energy supply — remains fully intact despite a $190 billion AI infrastructure capex push, directly challenging the narrative that Big Tech is retreating from granular clean energy standards. More critically for Indian companies, this reaffirmation arrives alongside the GHG Protocol's proposed shift to mandatory hourly matching and deliverability requirements for Scope 2 emissions reporting — a methodological change that would invalidate the annual Renewable Energy Certificate (REC) and bundled Power Purchase Agreement approach currently used by the vast majority of Indian listed companies to report zero or near-zero Scope 2 emissions in their BRSR disclosures. Indian CFOs and CSOs who believe their Scope 2 emissions position is clean under existing REC-backed claims must urgently reassess: the accounting floor is about to move, and global technology supply chain customers are already enforcing a stricter standard.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/google-exec-confirms-2030-carbon-free-energy-commitment-remains-in-place-despite-ai-buildout/) confirms that Google's 24/7 CFE goal — first announced in 2020 as a 'moonshot' target requiring carbon-free energy matching every hour of every day in every region of operation — has not been diluted, scaled back, or deferred, even as the company deploys $190 billion in AI data centre capex in 2025-26. This matters because peers, most notably Microsoft, are reportedly reconsidering their '100/100/0' equivalent commitment, and there was a reasonable market expectation that Google might follow suit given the power intensity of generative AI workloads. Google has held the line. The more structurally significant development embedded in this news is the explicit reference to the GHG Protocol's proposed hourly matching and deliverability requirement for Scope 2 emissions accounting. The current GHG Protocol Scope 2 Guidance (2015) permits two methods: the location-based method (grid average emission factors) and the market-based method (which allows RECs, GOs, and PPAs to claim zero Scope 2 emissions regardless of when the renewable energy was actually generated). The proposed revision would require that clean energy be matched to consumption on an hourly basis and be physically deliverable to the grid where consumption occurs — precisely what Google already does internally and what makes annual-REC-based claims inadequate by that standard. For Indian listed companies, this is not an abstract accounting debate. Under SEBI's BRSR Core framework — mandatory for the top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 and extending progressively to the top 1,000 — Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions are a Key Performance Indicator subject to reasonable assurance. The vast majority of Indian companies with renewable energy claims are using I-REC (International REC) certificates or CERC-registered RECs purchased on an annual basis to support market-based Scope 2 disclosures. If the GHG Protocol formalises hourly matching as the standard, those claims will be methodologically non-compliant with the framework their BRSR disclosures reference, creating audit exposure, investor pushback, and supply chain eligibility risk simultaneously.
who_is_affected: Indian data centre operators and co-location providers (Adani Enterprises, NTT India, STT GDC, CtrlS, Yotta Infrastructure) supplying compute capacity to hyperscaler clients with 24/7 CFE procurement requirements

IT services and BPO exporters (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCL Tech, Tech Mahindra) that operate large captive campuses and report Scope 2 emissions using annual I-REC purchases — a significant portion of their science-based targets and net zero commitments rest on this accounting

RE100 member companies with Indian operations — currently 44+ Indian companies are RE100 signatories — who must comply with RE100's technical criteria, which already recommends but does not yet mandate hourly matching

Top 1,000 BRSR-reporting listed companies with on-site or third-party renewable energy claims embedded in their market-based Scope 2 disclosures, particularly those subject to BRSR Core reasonable assurance
CFOs and Chief Sustainability Officers at BSE 500 companies where Scope 2 intensity metrics are tied to ESG-linked loan covenants, green bond frameworks, or sustainability-linked bond KPIs
ESG analysts and fund managers at domestic and foreign institutional investors (FIIs/DIIs) using BRSR Scope 2 data for portfolio carbon footprint reporting under SEBI's stewardship code expectations

Procurement and sustainability heads at Indian companies in the supply chains of Apple, Google, Microsoft, and Meta — all of whom have supplier-facing clean energy requirements that mirror or exceed 24/7 CFE standards
key_obligations: BRSR Core — Scope 2 GHG disclosure with reasonable assurance: Mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 reporting; extended to top 250 from FY2024-25; top 500 from FY2025-26; top 1,000 targeted by FY2026-27 — companies must specify the methodology (location-based vs. market-based) and ensure it is consistent with the GHG Protocol version they reference

GHG Protocol Scope 2 Guidance revision (proposed): The GHG Protocol has released a draft update for public consultation proposing hourly matching and deliverability as requirements for market-based Scope 2 claims; final standard expected in 2025-26 — Indian companies should assume adoption and begin gap assessment now

RE100 Technical Criteria update: RE100 is expected to align its criteria with any finalised GHG Protocol hourly matching standard — Indian RE100 signatories should engage The Climate Group's India desk to track timeline and compliance pathway

SBTi Corporate Net Zero Standard: Science-based targets validated by SBTi for Scope 2 that use market-based methods will need to be re-evaluated if the GHG Protocol baseline shifts; companies with SBTi-validated targets should flag this to their sustainability teams for revalidation planning

Google 24/7 CFE Supply Chain Requirement by 2030: Suppliers to Google's data centre and cloud infrastructure in India — including construction, cooling, IT hardware, and facilities management — will face contractual CFE verification requirements as 2030 approaches; supplier sustainability questionnaires will tighten from 2026 onward

BEE Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) Scheme Cycle VII: Energy-intensive data centre operators with a connected load exceeding 500 kW are increasingly being considered for inclusion in PAT; companies should track MoEFCC/BEE notifications for data centre sector designation
sections.climate_angle: Google's $190 billion AI capex commitment is a direct driver of energy demand in every country where it operates data centres — including its announced investments in India (Google has committed $2 billion to Indian cloud infrastructure with data centres in Mumbai and Pune). The tension King acknowledges — massive power demand from AI workloads versus 24/7 CFE requirements — is precisely the tension that Indian grid planners, state DISCOMs, and renewable energy developers must now design for. India's current grid cannot support 24/7 CFE at the granularity Google requires: the combination of intermittent solar and wind, inadequate battery storage, and grid balancing constraints means that hourly matching in, say, Maharashtra or Telangana is practically infeasible today without significant pumped hydro, green hydrogen peaking, or behind-the-meter storage. This creates a paradox for Indian renewable energy policy. MNRE's target of 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030 is calibrated around annual energy generation, not hourly deliverability. If global technology clients begin enforcing 24/7 CFE as a procurement standard — and Google's reaffirmation signals they will — India's renewable energy market structure, the CERC REC framework, the SECI auction design, and the open access regime must evolve toward time-of-day and location-specific matching. The BRSR framework, which currently accepts both GHG Protocol methods without preference, will need to specify methodology requirements as SEBI's ESG disclosure architecture matures toward ISSB IFRS S2 alignment. ISSB S2, for its reference to Scope 2 accounting, defers to the GHG Protocol — meaning a GHG Protocol shift to hourly matching automatically transmits into India's BRSR assurance framework, creating a regulatory cascade that most Indian boards have not yet modelled.
what_to_do: Sustainability / ESG teams (immediate, within 60 days): Audit all market-based Scope 2 claims in the most recent BRSR and any CDP or investor disclosures — identify specifically what instruments are used (I-RECs, Indian RECs, bundled PPAs, unbundled certificates) and whether they carry hourly or time-stamped generation data; document the gap versus the proposed GHG Protocol hourly matching standard

CFOs and Company Secretaries (within 90 days): Review ESG-linked financing agreements — green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, SLBs — for any KPI definitions referencing Scope 2 intensity or renewable energy percentage; assess whether a methodology change under GHG Protocol revision would trigger a KPI breach or require lender/bondholder notification under the agreement terms

Chief Sustainability Officers and Energy Procurement Teams (within 6 months): Engage SECI, CERC, and open access regulators to understand feasibility of time-stamped or hourly-attributed REC issuance in India; explore pilot 24/7 CFE PPA structures with wind-solar hybrid or storage-backed capacity — this is already technically feasible in some Round-the-Clock (RTC) tender formats SECI has issued

Board Risk Committees (next board cycle): Add 'Scope 2 methodology risk under GHG Protocol revision' as a named item on the climate risk register; instruct management to present a scenario analysis showing the Scope 2 emissions restated under hourly matching methodology versus current annual REC basis — for most Indian IT and manufacturing companies, this restatement will be material

IR and investor communications teams (before next AGM): Proactively disclose in investor presentations the methodology basis for Scope 2 claims and the company's awareness of the GHG Protocol revision; institutional investors — particularly those with TCFD or ISSB-aligned reporting requirements — are already asking this question in engagement meetings, and reactive disclosure is reputationally worse than proactive transparency

Supply chain and procurement heads at companies in hyperscaler supply chains (within 3 months): Request copies of Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta's current supplier sustainability requirements and cross-reference against your current energy attribute certificate holdings; identify contracts where a CFE clause is already embedded or likely to be added at renewal
sections.our_take: Google's reaffirmation of its 24/7 CFE commitment is not just a corporate communications moment — it is a market signal with durable regulatory and commercial consequences for Indian businesses that most boards have not yet fully processed. The AI buildout was supposed to be the escape hatch: the argument that massive, unavoidable energy demand would force a retreat from granular clean energy standards, giving the entire corporate sector permission to revert to annual averaging and offset-backed claims. Google has explicitly rejected that argument. When the world's largest data centre operator — deploying $190 billion in capex in a single year — says hourly matching remains the standard, it sets a procurement expectation that cascades down supply chains and ultimately shapes what institutional investors will accept in ESG disclosures. For India, the next 24 to 36 months will be decisive on two fronts. First, the GHG Protocol revision, if finalised with hourly matching as a requirement, will expose the methodological fragility of nearly every Indian listed company's current Scope 2 disclosure. SEBI will be forced to respond — either by specifying permissible Scope 2 methods in the BRSR framework explicitly, or by deferring to ISSB S2 alignment, which will itself incorporate the revised GHG Protocol. Either way, the REC-based annual averaging approach that has allowed hundreds of Indian companies to report impressively low Scope 2 figures will face a serious legitimacy challenge. Audit committees should be very uncomfortable with reasonable assurance opinions written on the current basis if the accounting standard beneath them is in active revision. Second, India's grid infrastructure and renewable energy market design must catch up. The Round-the-Clock tender format is a start, but MNRE and CERC need to move faster on time-stamped REC issuance, storage-backed PPA standards, and open access framework reforms that make 24/7 CFE commercially viable at scale. The companies and jurisdictions that solve the hourly matching problem first will win the next wave of hyperscaler data centre investment — and for India, that is a multi-billion dollar prize worth redesigning the regulatory architecture to capture.

posts.title: La Caisse's Novisto Investment Signals Institutional Capital Flowing Into ESG Data Infrastructure — What Indian CFOs Must Read Into This
posts.summary: Quebec's La Caisse — one of the world's largest institutional investors managing over CAD 450 billion — has made a strategic investment in Novisto, a Montreal-based ESG data management and reporting software platform, explicitly citing CSRD compliance pressure and California's climate disclosure law

sections.summary: Quebec's La Caisse — one of the world's largest institutional investors managing over CAD 450 billion — has made a strategic investment in Novisto, a Montreal-based ESG data management and reporting software platform, explicitly citing CSRD compliance pressure and California's climate disclosure laws as the commercial tailwinds. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, this is not a distant Canadian capital markets story: it is a direct signal that institutional investors globally are now treating auditable, structured ESG data infrastructure as a prerequisite for portfolio company resilience — a standard that SEBI's BRSR Core assurance mandate is already importing into India's top 150 listed companies. The confluence of mandatory third-party assurance under BRSR Core, the imminent pressure on Indian exporters from EU CSRD supply chain disclosure requirements, and the growing expectation from FII investors that Indian companies maintain GHG Protocol-aligned, audit-ready ESG data means Indian boards can no longer treat sustainability reporting as a compliance checkbox — it is becoming a capital access condition. Reported by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/la-caisse-invests-in-sustainability-reporting-software-provider-novisto/).
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today on 20 May 2026 reveals that La Caisse (formerly CDPQ) has made a fresh equity investment in Novisto, following the company's $27 million financing round in 2025 and its acquisition of carbon management software firm Minimum earlier in 2026. The strategic rationale articulated by La Caisse is unambiguous: companies face intensifying regulatory pressure to comply with sustainability reporting frameworks, and ESG data management software is now a critical piece of climate transition infrastructure — not an optional reporting aid. La Caisse's own 2025–2030 climate strategy sets a target of CAD 400 billion in 'climate action' investments by 2030, and its backing of Novisto is consistent with a thesis that data infrastructure enabling verifiable climate commitments is itself a climate solution worthy of institutional capital allocation. What makes this materially significant for India is the architecture of the problem Novisto solves. The platform enables companies to collect, manage, and report quantitative and qualitative ESG data across shifting frameworks — precisely the challenge that India's top 1,000 listed companies face under SEBI's BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report) regime. SEBI mandated BRSR Core disclosures — covering 49 KPIs across environment, social, and governance — with limited third-party assurance for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, expanding to the top 250 from FY2024-25 and beyond. The assurance requirement is the critical inflection point: once sustainability disclosures must be independently assured, the underlying data collection and management process faces audit-grade scrutiny. Indian companies relying on spreadsheets, email chains, and manually compiled sustainability reports are acutely exposed. The La Caisse–Novisto transaction is institutional validation that the global market has priced this risk — and is investing in the solution infrastructure accordingly.
who_is_affected: BSE/NSE top 250 listed companies subject to SEBI BRSR Core mandatory disclosures with assurance requirements — particularly in manufacturing, energy, metals, chemicals, and FMCG sectors with high Scope 1 and 2 emission intensity

Indian exporters to the EU — especially textiles, auto components, pharma, and steel — who face indirect CSRD supply chain disclosure obligations as tier-1 or tier-2 suppliers to large EU undertakings under ESRS standards

CFOs and Chief Sustainability Officers at listed Indian companies who own the intersection of financial and non-financial reporting, and who will face audit committee scrutiny on the quality of ESG data systems in FY2025-26 reporting cycles
Risk managers and company secretaries responsible for BRSR filing accuracy, who currently lack automated data lineage and audit trail capabilities that assured reporting demands

FII and institutional investors — including sovereign wealth funds, ESG-mandated pension funds, and DFIs like IFC and ADB — evaluating Indian portfolio companies for ESG data maturity as part of enhanced due diligence
Indian ESG software and consulting firms competing in or adjacent to the ESG data management space, for whom this transaction sets a valuation and capability benchmark
key_obligations: SEBI BRSR Core — top 150 listed companies: limited assurance on BRSR Core KPIs mandatory from FY2023-24 (already in effect); reasonable assurance phase-in expected as SEBI's roadmap matures — companies must have defensible data management systems in place now
SEBI BRSR Core — top 250 listed companies: mandatory BRSR Core disclosure (not yet full assurance) from FY2024-25; companies should treat this as an assurance-readiness preparation window

EU CSRD supply chain due diligence: Large EU undertakings must begin collecting supplier-level sustainability data under ESRS standards from FY2024 reporting (published 2025); Indian exporters supplying these companies face informal but commercially binding data disclosure requests starting now and hardening through 2026-2027

IFRS S1/S2 adoption: While India has not yet mandated ISSB standards, MCA and SEBI are actively monitoring convergence; companies reporting to global investors should align BRSR Core architecture with IFRS S2 climate disclosure requirements proactively

GHG Protocol Scope 3 — Indian listed companies with SBTi commitments or CDP disclosure obligations must ensure their ESG data platforms can support supply chain emissions data collection and third-party verification, a function Novisto-type platforms are purpose-built for
sections.climate_angle: La Caisse's investment thesis — that ESG data infrastructure is itself a climate transition enabler — maps directly onto India's own transition financing architecture. India's carbon market, currently structured under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified by MoEFCC in 2023 and operationalised under BEE's expanded PAT Scheme framework, will require participating companies to maintain verifiable, auditable emissions data. Without robust internal ESG data management systems, Indian companies cannot credibly participate in India's domestic carbon market, cannot substantiate SBTi-aligned reduction trajectories, and cannot respond to the quantitative ESG data requests embedded in BRSR Core Section A (GHG emissions — Scope 1, 2, and where applicable, Scope 3). The La Caisse–Novisto deal signals that the global institutional investment community is calibrating portfolio resilience not just on emissions targets, but on the quality of the data architecture behind those targets. Indian companies with CAD or EUR-denominated foreign borrowings, green bonds, or sustainability-linked loans (SLLs) should note that covenant compliance in these instruments increasingly references assured ESG data — not self-reported estimates. The credibility gap between Indian companies' climate commitments and the verifiability of their underlying data is the single most material ESG risk that Indian boards are currently underestimating.
what_to_do: CFO and CSO jointly — within Q1 FY2026-27: commission an ESG data infrastructure audit mapping current data collection processes against BRSR Core KPI requirements, identifying gaps in data lineage, audit trail, and version control that would not withstand limited assurance review

Company Secretary and Compliance team — immediately: review your statutory auditor's or assurance provider's expectations for BRSR Core assured disclosures; understand whether your current data management process (spreadsheet vs. platform) will be flagged as a material weakness in the assurance engagement

Procurement and Supply Chain heads at EU-exporting companies — within 6 months: map your top EU customer accounts to their CSRD reporting obligations; proactively engage on what ESG data they will require from you as a supplier under ESRS 2 and sector-specific ESRS standards, and begin platform-based data collection accordingly

Board Risk Committee — at next quarterly meeting: request a board-level briefing on ESG data governance maturity; frame this as an audit risk, a capital access risk (for SLL covenants, green bond eligibility, and FII mandates), and a regulatory compliance risk under SEBI BRSR Core assurance requirements

Investor Relations team — in next earnings cycle or ESG investor briefing: proactively address the quality and auditability of your ESG data systems; institutional investors familiar with the La Caisse–Novisto thesis will increasingly differentiate between companies with structured ESG data platforms and those without
sections.our_take: The La Caisse–Novisto transaction is a data point in a much larger pattern that Indian boards should read with urgency: the global institutional investment community has crossed an inflection point where ESG data quality is no longer a reporting nicety but a portfolio risk variable. When one of the world's largest pension-style investors deploys capital specifically into ESG data infrastructure — and explicitly cites regulatory pressure from CSRD and US state-level climate disclosure laws — it is telling us that the cost of ESG data weakness is now priced into capital allocation decisions. India is not insulated from this shift. SEBI's BRSR Core assurance mandate is the domestic regulatory forcing function, but the more commercially immediate pressure is coming from two directions simultaneously: EU CSRD supply chain obligations affecting Indian exporters from 2025-2026 onwards, and FII investor due diligence standards that are rapidly converging with ISSB IFRS S1/S2 expectations. In the next 12–24 months, I expect SEBI to expand the BRSR Core assurance perimeter beyond the top 250 companies, potentially to the top 500 or 1,000 listed entities, and to introduce reasonable assurance (not just limited assurance) requirements for the largest companies. Simultaneously, MCA's work on India's sustainability accounting standards — informed by ISSB — will create a third layer of data demand. Companies that have not invested in structured ESG data management platforms by FY2027 will face a compounding liability: assurance qualifications, investor engagement deficits, and supply chain disqualification from EU-linked value chains. The Indian ESG software market — currently fragmented between global platforms, mid-size Indian providers, and in-house Excel models — is ripe for the kind of institutional capital consolidation that the Novisto investment exemplifies. Indian PE and growth investors should take note: the next ESG infrastructure investment story will very likely be made in India.

posts.title: Pentagreen's $800M GIP Close: What the FAST-P Blended Finance Architecture Means for India's Transition Infrastructure Capital Gap
posts.summary: Pentagreen Capital — the HSBC and Temasek-backed debt financing platform — has reached an $800 million second close for its Green Investments Partnership (GIP), a blended finance vehicle operating under Singapore's MAS-anchored Financing Asia's Transition Partnership (FAST-P). Critically for Indian

sections.summary: Pentagreen Capital — the HSBC and Temasek-backed debt financing platform — has reached an $800 million second close for its Green Investments Partnership (GIP), a blended finance vehicle operating under Singapore's MAS-anchored Financing Asia's Transition Partnership (FAST-P). Critically for Indian boards and CFOs, GIP's mandate explicitly covers South Asia alongside Southeast Asia, deploying debt capital into renewable energy, EV infrastructure, sustainable transport, and water and waste management — sectors where India's transition project pipeline is enormous but commercial financing remains structurally underpenetrated. This signals that institutional-grade blended finance capital is moving toward India's transition infrastructure at scale, with direct implications for how Indian project developers, listed infrastructure companies, and their lenders should position themselves for access. Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/hsbc-temasek-backed-pentagreen-raises-800-million-for-asia-focused-green-transition-fund/) covers the second close announcement in full.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today, the GIP fund has grown from its September 2025 first close of $510 million to $800 million at second close, with new institutional lenders DBS Bank and Cathay United Bank entering senior tranche positions — a meaningful signal of expanding commercial bank appetite for blended finance structures in the Asian transition space. The fund's tiered capital architecture — separating junior (concessional/philanthropic) and senior (commercial) tranches — is specifically designed to de-risk the 'marginally bankable' category of climate infrastructure that traditional project finance cannot absorb at current risk-adjusted return thresholds. This is precisely where India's transition pipeline sits: sub-investment-grade renewable projects in Tier 2 and Tier 3 markets, early-stage EV charging networks, distributed water treatment, and industrial energy transition projects that lack the balance sheet strength or offtake certainty to attract pure commercial debt. The India-specific consequence is structural, not incidental. India faces a documented climate finance gap estimated at over $170 billion annually through 2030 to meet its NDC commitments under the Paris Agreement, including its 500 GW non-fossil capacity target and energy intensity reduction goals. Domestic DFIs — IREDA, REC Ltd, PFC — have expanded their books aggressively, but their capital is concentrated in large-scale, utility-grade renewable projects with sovereign or near-sovereign credit profiles. The 'missing middle' — mid-market transition projects, industrial decarbonisation, green mobility, climate-resilient water infrastructure — remains severely underfunded. GIP's mandate, architecture, and sectoral focus map directly onto this gap. Indian project developers, green infrastructure SPVs, and transition-focused companies that can meet GIP's ESG and technical eligibility criteria now have a credible new debt capital pathway, provided they can demonstrate bankable environmental integrity under frameworks that GIP's LP base — which includes development finance institutions and institutional asset managers — will require. For Indian listed companies, this matters at the strategic level. Access to blended finance pools like GIP increasingly requires alignment with internationally recognised disclosure standards. GIP's LP base, including development finance institutions linked to MAS's FAST-P architecture, will apply ESG due diligence screens consistent with IFC Performance Standards, TCFD-aligned climate risk disclosure, and emerging ISSB IFRS S1/S2 requirements. Indian companies seeking to tap this capital — or seeking co-financing from GIP counterparties — will face diligence requirements that go substantially beyond current BRSR Core thresholds. The fund's DBS participation is also notable: DBS has explicitly aligned its own sustainable finance taxonomy with MAS's taxonomy and Singapore Green Finance standards, meaning project eligibility for GIP debt will be assessed against criteria that Indian companies may need to prepare for in advance.
who_is_affected: Indian renewable energy developers — utility scale and mid-market — seeking project debt for solar, wind, storage, and hybrid assets that fall below investment-grade thresholds without credit enhancement
EV infrastructure companies and fleet electrification platforms building out charging networks and electric bus/truck fleets across Indian Tier 1 and Tier 2 cities
Water and waste management infrastructure developers — including those operating under CPCB EPR frameworks for plastic and municipal solid waste — where blended finance can bridge the viability gap

Listed Indian infrastructure companies (BSE 500, NSE-listed) with transition project pipelines that require long-tenor, competitively priced debt and are currently reliant on domestic DFI financing alone
CFOs and treasury heads at Indian conglomerates with dedicated green infrastructure subsidiaries or SPV structures seeking to diversify international debt capital sources

Chief Sustainability Officers at Indian companies preparing BRSR Core disclosures who need to align their ESG reporting architecture with international investor-grade standards to qualify for blended finance diligence
Indian private infrastructure funds and green bond issuers who may seek co-investment, leverage, or anchor debt relationships with GIP-style vehicles

ESG analysts and institutional investors at FPIs and domestic funds assessing Indian infrastructure companies' access to concessional and blended international capital as a valuation and growth driver
key_obligations: BRSR Core — mandatory for India's top 150 listed entities by market cap from FY2023-24, expanding to top 250 from FY2024-25: Companies seeking international blended finance must treat BRSR Core as a floor, not a ceiling — GIP-level diligence will require Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 GHG data, water intensity metrics, and transition plan disclosures that go beyond current BRSR minimums

ISSB IFRS S1 and S2 — while not yet mandatory in India, SEBI's International Advisory Board and MCA have both signalled convergence intent: Indian companies approaching GIP or FAST-P-linked financiers should begin voluntary ISSB-aligned climate risk disclosure now, as DFI LP due diligence will apply these standards as a reference framework from 2025 onwards

IFC Performance Standards (PS1–PS8) — any project receiving GIP debt financing must demonstrate Environmental and Social Management System (ESMS) compliance; Indian project developers must assess their current ESMS maturity against IFC PS requirements before approaching GIP-linked intermediaries

India Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) 2023 — BEE-notified obligated entities in energy-intensive sectors pursuing transition projects should map their CCTS compliance trajectory, as blended finance eligibility for industrial decarbonisation may increasingly require demonstrated carbon accounting credibility

RBI's Draft Framework for Sustainable Finance (under consultation) — Indian banks acting as on-lenders or co-financiers in GIP-style structures will need to align their own green finance taxonomies with RBI guidance once finalised, expected to reference MAS and EU taxonomy principles

Green transition project developers must prepare bankable project documentation consistent with international ESG standards — including climate risk assessments, biodiversity impact screening under TNFD-adjacent frameworks, and gender and community safeguard disclosures — before seeking GIP debt eligibility
sections.climate_angle: The GIP architecture is a direct institutional response to what climate finance practitioners call the 'transition finance gap' — distinct from the green finance gap. Green finance covers already-bankable, proven-technology renewable assets. Transition finance covers the harder, messier middle ground: coal plant retirements with just transition obligations, industrial process decarbonisation, early-stage green mobility at non-metropolitan scale, and climate-resilient water infrastructure in high-physical-risk geographies. India's climate transition pathway under its Updated NDC — targeting 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030, a 45% reduction in emissions intensity, and net zero by 2070 — is overwhelmingly a transition finance challenge, not a green finance challenge. The bankable large-scale solar and wind market is reasonably well-served. The transition finance market is not. GIP's blended structure directly addresses this by using junior/concessional capital to absorb first-loss risk and unlock senior commercial debt at scale — the same logic that underpins India's own IREDA viability gap funding mechanisms and the World Bank's partial risk guarantee instruments deployed in Indian renewable auctions. What GIP adds is scale, international LP diversity, and explicit alignment with FAST-P's multilateral anchoring under MAS, which gives the instrument a credibility and replicability that purely bilateral DFI instruments lack. For Indian companies, the climate transition angle is also a BRSR Core obligation angle. SEBI's BRSR Core requires top-listed companies to disclose transition risks under Principle 6 of the National Guidelines on Responsible Business Conduct (NGRBC). Companies that cannot demonstrate a credible transition plan — with quantified Scope 1 and 2 emissions, energy intensity reduction trajectories, and capex allocation toward low-carbon assets — will not only face investor scrutiny under BRSR but will also be functionally ineligible for GIP-style international transition finance. The ESG reporting architecture and the capital access architecture are now the same architecture. The ISSB S2 standard's concept of 'climate-related opportunities' — specifically transition opportunities arising from the shift to lower-carbon energy systems — is precisely what GIP is designed to fund. Indian companies that have begun ISSB S2-aligned scenario analysis and identified their transition opportunity pipeline are already better positioned to engage with GIP-linked capital intermediaries. Those that have not begun this work are creating a capital access risk, not merely a disclosure gap.
what_to_do: CFO / Treasury (immediate — within 60 days): Map your company's transition project pipeline against GIP's stated investment mandate — renewable energy and storage, EV infrastructure, sustainable transport, water and waste management — and identify which projects may qualify for international blended finance debt. Engage your investment banking relationships with HSBC and DBS (both are GIP participants) to understand deal origination pathways and eligibility thresholds

Chief Sustainability Officer (next 90 days): Conduct a gap assessment between your current BRSR Core disclosures and IFC Performance Standards PS1–PS8 requirements, including ESMS maturity, climate risk disclosure depth, and Scope 3 coverage. GIP-level ESG diligence will exceed BRSR minimum requirements — identify and close those gaps before approaching international DFI-linked capital

Board Risk Committee (next board cycle): Commission a formal briefing on India's transition finance landscape — including FAST-P, GIP, and comparable instruments such as Climate Policy Initiative's blended finance vehicles and AIIB co-financing programs — and approve a capital diversification strategy that explicitly includes international blended finance as a funding source for climate transition capex

Sustainability / IR team (within 6 months): Begin voluntary ISSB IFRS S2 disclosure preparation, including physical and transition climate risk assessment using scenario analysis (1.5°C and 2°C pathways). Frame this as both a regulatory readiness measure (SEBI convergence is coming) and an investor relations priority for FPI capital retention

Legal / Compliance (ongoing): Monitor RBI's Sustainable Finance Framework consultation for guidance on how Indian bank co-lenders in blended finance structures will be regulated — this will determine the availability of domestic bank participation in GIP-style co-financing arrangements for Indian projects

Project development teams at infrastructure companies: Initiate TNFD-aligned biodiversity and ecosystem impact screening for transition projects in ecologically sensitive areas — GIP's LP base includes development finance institutions that will require this as a condition precedent for debt disbursement
sections.our_take: The Pentagreen GIP second close is not a fund story — it is an architecture story. What MAS has built through FAST-P, and what GIP operationalises, is a replicable institutional blueprint for mobilising private capital into transition finance at the scale Asia needs. The fact that DBS — a fully commercial, regulator-supervised bank — has entered as a senior tranche lender is the single most important data point in this announcement. It signals that the risk-return recalibration that blended finance is designed to achieve is actually working at institutional scale, not just in pilot structures. When commercial banks begin treating transition infrastructure debt as a core asset class rather than a developmental accommodation, the capital mobilisation dynamic changes fundamentally. For India, the 12–36 month implication is this: SEBI, RBI, and MoEFCC are all moving — at different speeds — toward a regulatory architecture that will make ESG disclosure quality a direct determinant of capital access, not merely a compliance exercise. SEBI's BRSR Core is the first layer. RBI's sustainable finance taxonomy, when finalised, will be the second. India's participation in ISSB convergence will be the third. Companies that treat these frameworks as independent compliance obligations are misreading the trajectory. The international capital market — and specifically blended finance vehicles like GIP, with their explicit South Asia mandates — is already running ahead of domestic regulatory timelines. Indian companies that invest now in ISSB-aligned disclosure, IFC-standard ESMS, and bankable transition project documentation will have a measurable cost-of-capital advantage over peers that wait for regulatory compulsion. Boards that understand this are making a strategic capital allocation decision. Boards that do not are accumulating an unpriced risk. Watch for SEBI to reference blended finance eligibility criteria in its forthcoming ESG disclosure consultations — the regulator is closely tracking how international capital is pricing Indian ESG quality, and the feedback loop between disclosure standards and capital access is tightening faster than most Indian companies currently appreciate.

posts.title: Novata's Risk Atlas: Why AI-Powered Supply Chain Risk Monitoring for Private Markets Has Direct Implications for Indian PE-Backed Companies and BRSR Value Chain Disclosures
posts.summary: Novata's launch of Risk Atlas — an AI-powered, multi-category ESG risk monitoring tool for private markets portfolios and supply chains — signals a structural shift in how global GPs and LPs will assess, screen, and engage with portfolio companies on ESG risk, including those domiciled in India. For

sections.summary: Novata's launch of Risk Atlas — an AI-powered, multi-category ESG risk monitoring tool for private markets portfolios and supply chains — signals a structural shift in how global GPs and LPs will assess, screen, and engage with portfolio companies on ESG risk, including those domiciled in India. For Indian companies that are either PE-backed, export-oriented, or sit within the supply chains of listed multinationals, this is not a distant fintech story: it is a preview of the due diligence standard their international investors will apply within the next 12–24 months. Combined with SEBI's BRSR Core value chain disclosure requirement — which mandates India's top 250 listed companies to obtain ESG disclosures from their top suppliers and customers — Risk Atlas-class tools are precisely the infrastructure that global LPs will use to flag, score, and escalate Indian counterparty risk before capital deployment or during portfolio review.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/novata-launches-portfolio-supply-chain-risk-monitoring-solution/) covers the May 2026 launch of Risk Atlas by Novata — a New York-based sustainability data platform co-founded by S&P Global, the Ford Foundation, Hamilton Lane, and Omidyar Network. Risk Atlas uses AI to continuously surface, structure, and refresh risk signals across five categories: reputational, cyber, geopolitical, physical climate, and transition risk. The platform is designed for the full investment lifecycle — from pre-investment screening through ongoing portfolio monitoring — and allows GPs and LPs to compare risk exposure across companies, sectors, and geographies, set custom thresholds, and prioritise engagement where material exposure is highest. What this overturns is the older model of annual ESG questionnaires and static ESG scores. Risk Atlas represents a move to continuous, AI-refreshed, multi-dimensional risk surveillance — a category of tool that will increasingly define the minimum standard of ESG due diligence expected by sophisticated LPs in North America and Europe. For Indian context, this has three direct pressure points. First, Indian PE-backed companies — particularly in sectors like manufacturing, logistics, pharma, chemicals, and consumer goods — will increasingly face this level of scrutiny from their foreign PE sponsors and co-investors. Second, Indian companies that are sub-suppliers to global listed multinationals subject to EU CSRD or the German Supply Chain Due Diligence Act (LkSG) are already within scope of value chain ESG risk mapping; Risk Atlas accelerates the commercial implementation of that obligation. Third, and most critically for Indian listed companies, SEBI's BRSR Core framework (effective FY2023-24 for top 150 listed companies, extending to top 250 from FY2024-25) already requires value chain ESG disclosures — meaning Indian CFOs and CSOs are building the data infrastructure that tools like Risk Atlas will query and benchmark against global peers.
who_is_affected: PE-backed Indian companies in manufacturing, pharma, chemicals, logistics, textiles, and consumer goods — particularly those with foreign GP or LP ownership via APAC-focused or global private equity funds
BSE 500 and NSE-listed companies in the top 250 by market capitalisation, subject to SEBI BRSR Core value chain disclosure requirements from FY2024-25 onwards

Indian exporters supplying to EU, UK, or US multinationals subject to CSRD, LkSG, or the forthcoming UK sustainability disclosure standards — these buyers will use tools like Risk Atlas to assess sub-supplier ESG risk continuously

CFOs and CSOs at Indian conglomerates with complex multi-tier supply chains, who must now anticipate that their international investors are moving from point-in-time ESG scoring to continuous risk monitoring
ESG and IR teams at Indian listed companies that are either investee companies or supply chain nodes for global asset managers running PE or infrastructure funds through platforms like Hamilton Lane

Institutional investors — domestic MFs, insurance companies, and FPIs — that have exposure to Indian PE-backed unlisted companies and need equivalent risk surveillance tools for their own stewardship reporting under SEBI's stewardship code
key_obligations: BRSR Core — Value Chain Disclosure: Top 250 NSE/BSE-listed companies (by market cap) must disclose ESG performance of value chain partners (upstream and downstream) covering at least 75% of purchases/sales by value — effective FY2024-25 annual reports, filed by September 2025. Companies that have not yet operationalised supplier ESG data collection are already in a deficit position relative to what global investors using Risk Atlas-class tools will expect.

ISSB IFRS S1/S2 — Supply Chain Risk Disclosure: ISSB standards require entities to disclose material sustainability risks across the value chain, including Scope 3 emissions, physical climate risk, and transition risk. Indian companies with international listings or cross-listed bonds, or those raising ECB/green bonds, face de facto adoption pressure as global investors align their due diligence to ISSB.

EU CSRD / ESRS E1 and S2: Indian suppliers to EU-listed companies with turnover above €150M must provide ESG data to their EU customers under ESRS S2 (Own Workforce) and E1 (Climate) from FY2025 reporting cycles. This is a contractual, not just regulatory, obligation — and non-compliant Indian suppliers face delistment from preferred vendor programs.

German Supply Chain Due Diligence Act (LkSG): Already in force for German companies with 1,000+ employees since January 2024. Indian sub-suppliers in the automotive, textile, and chemical sectors are within scope of LkSG risk assessments — which Risk Atlas-type tools are purpose-built to operationalise.

SEBI Stewardship Code (2019, revised 2020): Institutional investors holding equity in listed Indian companies are expected to engage on material ESG risks, including supply chain risks. Continuous AI-powered risk monitoring creates both the data and the obligation trigger for escalated engagement.
sections.climate_angle: Risk Atlas's inclusion of physical climate risk and transition risk as two of its five monitoring categories is directly aligned with the ISSB IFRS S2 disclosure architecture, which Indian listed companies will increasingly be benchmarked against by global asset allocators. For India, physical climate risk is not abstract: the RBI's 2023 report on climate risk and the finance sector explicitly flagged that Indian banks and institutional investors carry significant unpriced physical climate exposure in their portfolios, particularly in coastal manufacturing, agriculture-linked supply chains, and infrastructure. A tool that continuously monitors physical climate risk signals at the portfolio and supply chain level will, within 24 months, become the standard by which foreign LPs assess whether their Indian GP partners are adequately managing climate-related financial risk under TCFD-aligned fiduciary standards. On transition risk, Indian companies in carbon-intensive sectors — steel, cement, aluminium, petrochemicals — are simultaneously subject to India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 and to the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), which enters its full charging phase from January 2026. Risk Atlas's transition risk module will be used by European investors and buyers to flag Indian exporters with high CBAM exposure or inadequate decarbonisation roadmaps, directly affecting their access to European trade finance and supply chain contracts. Indian companies that have not yet mapped their transition risk under a Paris-aligned scenario — as required under BRSR Core's TCFD-aligned disclosures — will appear as elevated-risk nodes in any Risk Atlas-class assessment.
what_to_do: CFO / Treasury (Immediate — within 60 days): Audit your current ESG data collection architecture against what Risk Atlas-class platforms will query — specifically: Do you have quantified, audit-ready data on Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 emissions? Do you have supplier-level ESG scorecards covering at least 75% of procurement value, as required under BRSR Core? Gaps here will directly surface as 'data unavailable' risk flags in LP-level monitoring tools.

CSO / Sustainability Team (Within 90 days): Map your company's exposure across Risk Atlas's five risk categories — reputational, cyber, geopolitical, physical climate, and transition risk — and document where Indian-specific risk factors apply (e.g., CBAM exposure for exports, monsoon-related physical risk for operations, supply chain geopolitical risk from China+1 procurement shifts). Use this as the basis for your BRSR Core materiality assessment and your ISSB S1/S2 gap analysis.

Procurement / Supply Chain Head (Within 6 months): Design and deploy a supplier ESG data collection programme that meets BRSR Core's value chain disclosure threshold. Prioritise Tier-1 suppliers in carbon-intensive or socially high-risk categories. Align questionnaire design with GRI 308/414 and ESRS S2 to ensure data collected is usable for both Indian regulatory reporting and EU customer due diligence.

Board Risk Committee (Next board cycle): Commission a briefing on how global LPs and GPs invested in or co-investing alongside the company are evolving their ESG risk monitoring practices — specifically whether they have deployed or plan to deploy continuous AI-based monitoring tools. Ensure the board's risk register explicitly captures BRSR value chain disclosure risk, CBAM transition risk, and physical climate risk as named categories with owners and mitigation plans.

IR / ESG Investor Relations (Within 90 days): Proactively communicate to foreign institutional investors and PE co-investors the status of your BRSR Core compliance, value chain ESG data programme, and any third-party assurance obtained on ESG disclosures. Investors using Risk Atlas-class tools will flag companies with opaque or unavailable ESG data as higher risk — a proactive IR position can influence how your company is scored.
sections.our_take: The launch of Risk Atlas is one data point in a much larger structural shift: the industrialisation of ESG risk intelligence for private markets. For too long, ESG due diligence in PE and private credit was episodic, questionnaire-driven, and manually intensive — which allowed Indian PE-backed and unlisted companies to operate with significant ESG data opacity relative to their listed peers. AI-powered continuous monitoring tools eliminate that opacity at scale. Within the next 18–36 months, we expect that any serious global GP with Indian portfolio exposure will either build or license a Risk Atlas-class capability, and LP advisory committees will begin requiring evidence of such monitoring as part of fund ESG governance standards — particularly as ILPA's ESG reporting guidance continues to harden and as EU AIFMD revisions tighten ESG disclosure obligations on EU-domiciled fund managers with Indian portfolio assets. For Indian regulators, this trajectory has a direct policy implication: SEBI's BRSR Core value chain disclosure framework was prescient, but its enforcement and assurance architecture needs to mature rapidly. The current requirement for 'reasonable assurance' on BRSR Core KPIs from FY2023-24 is a necessary foundation, but the assurance standards applied by Indian chartered accountants and sustainability assurance providers are not yet calibrated to the granularity that tools like Risk Atlas will expect. MoEFCC and SEBI should consider whether India's PAT scheme data, CPCB EPR compliance registry, and BEE energy performance data can be made machine-readable and API-accessible — which would allow Indian companies to demonstrate regulatory compliance as a positive risk signal within global ESG monitoring platforms, rather than having their risk profile defined entirely by the absence of data. Indian companies that invest now in structured, audit-ready, API-compatible ESG data infrastructure will have a material competitive advantage in capital markets and supply chain positioning within three years. Those that do not will find themselves flagged, downweighted, or excluded — not by a regulator, but by an algorithm.

posts.title: Datamaran Core's CSRD/ISSB Enhancements: What AI-Driven Double Materiality and IRO Gap Analysis Mean for Indian Companies Navigating BRSR Core and Global Disclosure Convergence
posts.summary: Datamaran's May 2026 enhancements to its AI-powered Datamaran Core platform — including a Double Materiality IRO gap analysis benchmarked against audited CSRD peer reports, dynamic stakeholder intelligence, and AI-generated disclosure recommendations — represent a material shift in how technology is

sections.summary: Datamaran's May 2026 enhancements to its AI-powered Datamaran Core platform — including a Double Materiality IRO gap analysis benchmarked against audited CSRD peer reports, dynamic stakeholder intelligence, and AI-generated disclosure recommendations — represent a material shift in how technology is industrialising sustainability compliance. For Indian listed companies, this matters immediately on two fronts: Indian subsidiaries and supply chain partners of EU-domiciled groups are already inside the CSRD value chain scope under ESRS 2 and sector-specific standards, and SEBI's BRSR Core framework — which mandates assurance for the top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 — is converging structurally with the ISSB IFRS S1/S2 logic that Datamaran Core now explicitly supports. The emergence of institutionalised, AI-benchmarked IRO gap analysis raises the bar for what 'adequate' sustainability disclosure looks like — and Indian boards unprepared for that standard face growing audit exposure and investor scrutiny.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/datamaran-launches-new-tools-to-support-csrd-issb-compliance-governance/) covers Datamaran's announcement on 20 May 2026 of a substantive upgrade to Datamaran Core, its AI-powered risk and governance platform. The enhancements are not cosmetic. The platform now delivers: (1) external signal monitoring tracking regulatory developments, peer disclosures, and value chain changes in near real-time; (2) a Dynamic Stakeholder Intelligence Matrix that maps evolving stakeholder priorities — a direct operational response to the ESRS 2 General Disclosures requirement for documented stakeholder engagement; (3) enhanced collaboration architecture with role-based workflows and stakeholder surveys that create an auditable trail across business functions; (4) an IRO gap analysis view benchmarked against audited CSRD peer reports — this is the critical differentiator, as it uses actual assurance-level peer filings as the benchmark, not aspirational frameworks; and (5) AI-generated recommendations grounded in peer disclosures rather than generic guidance. What this platform update overturns is the assumption that sustainability disclosure is primarily a reporting exercise managed by a small ESG team working from a materiality assessment done once every two to three years. Datamaran Core is architecting continuous, governed, cross-functional disclosure management — the same operating model that financial reporting has used for decades. For Indian companies, the India-specific implication is structural: SEBI's BRSR Core — which requires the top 150 BSE/NSE-listed entities to provide reasonable assurance over 49 Key Performance Indicators from FY2023-24 onward, expanding to the top 250 from FY2024-25 — creates an assurance obligation that is functionally analogous to CSRD's limited assurance requirement under ESRS. Indian companies that treat BRSR Core as a compliance form-fill exercise rather than a governed, auditable process are creating exactly the kind of IRO gap that Datamaran's benchmarking tool is now designed to expose — and that institutional investors and Big Four auditors are increasingly equipped to identify.
who_is_affected: Indian subsidiaries of EU-headquartered multinationals: directly inside CSRD value chain scope under ESRS G1, E1, and S1/S2 — parent-level CSRD filings now require supplier and subsidiary-level data that Indian entities must furnish

Top 250 BSE/NSE-listed Indian companies subject to SEBI BRSR Core assurance obligations — particularly those in manufacturing, IT services, FMCG, chemicals, and infrastructure where IRO identification across Scope 1/2/3 and social dimensions is complex

Indian exporters to the EU in textiles, pharma, auto components, and steel — where CBAM (for steel and aluminium), EUDR (for forest-linked commodities), and supply chain due diligence under CSDDD create compounding disclosure demands that feed into CSRD value chain chapters
CFOs at listed Indian companies who own the BRSR sign-off and are increasingly accountable to audit committees for the quality and assurance-readiness of sustainability KPIs
Chief Sustainability Officers and sustainability heads who must now defend materiality assessments against audited peer benchmarks — not just internal judgement

ESG and climate-focused institutional investors — domestic (SBI MF, HDFC AMC, Mirae, Edelweiss Alternatives) and foreign (Norges Bank, APG, CDPQ) — who hold concentrated positions in BSE 500 companies and are using platform-level data to assess disclosure quality gaps
Big Four audit firms and sustainability assurance providers in India whose BRSR Core assurance engagements will increasingly be benchmarked against CSRD-equivalent rigour
key_obligations: SEBI BRSR Core — Top 150 listed entities: reasonable assurance over 49 KPIs mandatory from FY2023-24 annual reports (filed by September 2024); top 250 entities from FY2024-25 — sustainability teams must ensure IRO identification is audit-ready, not retrospective

EU CSRD — Large EU-listed companies (500+ employees): first CSRD-compliant reports covering FY2024 were due in 2025; mid-size listed EU companies covering FY2025 report in 2026 — Indian subsidiaries inside these groups must already be supplying structured Scope 3 and social data upstream

ISSB IFRS S1/S2 — Jurisdictions adopting ISSB (Australia, UK, Singapore, Canada, Japan confirmed; India's ICAI and MCA have signalled convergence): Indian companies with cross-listed securities or overseas bond issuances face growing ISSB-aligned disclosure expectations from lenders and rating agencies from 2025-2026 onward

ESRS value chain disclosure (ESRS 2, SBM-3): EU parent companies must disclose material impacts and risks in their upstream value chains — Indian contract manufacturers, IT vendors, and raw material suppliers must respond to data requests from EU clients or risk supply chain delisting

BRSR Core Scope 3 KPI (KPI 22 — value chain GHG emissions): listed Indian companies must begin disclosing Scope 3 categories relevant to their business model — a requirement that demands the same IRO mapping logic that Datamaran Core now automates
sections.climate_angle: The climate transition relevance of Datamaran Core's upgrade goes beyond software features. The IRO gap analysis benchmarked against audited CSRD peer reports operationalises something that Indian boards have been slow to internalise: climate-related risks and opportunities are no longer self-assessed in isolation — they are now benchmarked against what peer companies have disclosed under external assurance. This creates a ratchet effect. Once CSRD-reporting peers in a sector establish a disclosure baseline — say, a European steel company disclosing transition plan milestones, carbon price sensitivities, and Scope 3 category-specific decarbonisation pathways under ESRS E1 — every Indian steel company supplying into that value chain, or competing for the same green bond investor pool, is implicitly being measured against that baseline. For Indian companies specifically, this connects to three live pressure points. First, the RBI's 2024 climate risk disclosure guidelines for banks and NBFCs, which require financial institutions to assess borrower-level transition and physical risk — a process that depends on the quality of ESG disclosures from listed borrowers. Second, SEBI's BRSR Core climate KPIs, which require Scope 1, 2, and relevant Scope 3 emissions with intensity metrics — the same data architecture that feeds ISSB IFRS S2 climate-related financial disclosures. Third, India's domestic carbon market under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified by MoEFCC in 2023 and operationalised through BEE's PAT scheme expansion — where credible emissions accounting, the foundation of any IRO-based climate risk analysis, determines both compliance cost and credit monetisation potential. Companies that build governed, continuously updated IRO registers now are building the data infrastructure that the CCTS, BRSR Core, and ISSB convergence will all eventually draw from.
what_to_do: Sustainability and ESG teams (immediate, within 30 days): map your company's current materiality assessment against the ESRS Double Materiality structure — identify whether your IRO register distinguishes between impact materiality and financial materiality, as SEBI's BRSR Core and ISSB S1/S2 both implicitly require this granularity for assurance readiness

CFOs and Finance teams (within 60 days): commission a gap analysis comparing your BRSR Core KPI disclosures against audited CSRD peer reports in your sector — this is exactly the benchmarking Datamaran Core now automates, but it can be done manually as a one-time diagnostic; the output should feed directly into your FY2025-26 BRSR Core assurance engagement scope

Legal and Compliance teams (within 90 days): identify which EU-headquartered clients, parent companies, or joint venture partners have CSRD obligations for FY2025 reporting — map what supplier-level data they will formally request from your Indian entity under ESRS 2 SBM-3 and ESRS G1 supply chain provisions, and establish a data governance protocol now rather than responding reactively

Board Risk and Audit Committees (next board cycle): include a standing agenda item on sustainability disclosure governance — specifically, whether the company's materiality process is documented, cross-functionally reviewed, and defensible under external assurance; reference SEBI's BRSR Core assurance obligation and the growing use of AI benchmarking tools by institutional investors to assess disclosure quality

Procurement and Supply Chain heads (within 90 days): for companies that are suppliers to EU-listed groups, proactively build a structured ESG data response capability — scope, emissions intensity, water use, human rights due diligence — before your EU clients formalise their CSRD value chain data requests; being ahead of the request strengthens commercial relationships and reduces delisting risk

Investor Relations teams (ongoing): brief your top 20 institutional investors on your BRSR Core assurance status, your IRO identification methodology, and your ISSB convergence roadmap — as platforms like Datamaran Core become standard tools for investor-side ESG analysis, companies that cannot articulate a structured IRO framework will face increasing ESG rating downgrades and exclusion from sustainability-themed funds
sections.our_take: Datamaran Core's upgrade is a signal, not just a product announcement. It marks the point at which sustainability disclosure governance is being industrialised the same way that financial reporting controls were industrialised after Sarbanes-Oxley — through technology platforms that create auditable, role-governed, continuously updated data trails benchmarked against external assurance-level comparators. The implication for Indian boards over the next 24-36 months is severe and underappreciated: the standard of 'adequate' ESG disclosure is about to be set not by regulatory minimums but by what AI-benchmarked peer analysis identifies as the norm among assurance-level CSRD reporters. Indian companies that are still treating BRSR Core as a form-filling compliance exercise — rather than as a governed, enterprise-wide disclosure process — will find themselves on the wrong side of that benchmark as institutional investors, Big Four auditors, and ESG rating agencies all adopt similar AI-driven gap analysis tools. Three specific regulatory and capital market shifts warrant board-level attention in the next 12-36 months. First, SEBI is likely to expand BRSR Core assurance obligations beyond the top 250 listed entities and progressively tighten the KPI scope — the ministry's own 2023 consultation papers and IOSCO's global push for ISSB adoption signal that India's trajectory is toward reasonable assurance over a full ISSB S1/S2-equivalent disclosure set by the end of the decade. Second, India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, once operationalised beyond the PAT sector, will create a direct financial consequence for companies with weak IRO-level emissions accounting — the quality of your climate risk identification today determines your CCTS compliance cost tomorrow. Third, the EU's CSDDD (Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive), now in force with transposition deadlines through 2026-2027, will extend legally binding supply chain due diligence obligations to large EU companies' Indian suppliers — meaning that Indian manufacturers who cannot produce structured IRO registers, human rights assessments, and environmental impact disclosures will face contractual and legal exposure, not just reputational risk. Boards that treat ESG disclosure governance as a cost centre are mispricing the liability they are accumulating.

posts.title: CPCB Extends Interim Relief for Importers of 85 EEE Items: EPR Registration Deadline Now 30 June 2023
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a clarificatory letter dated 9 May 2023 to the Customs authorities, extending interim arrangements for releasing imported consignments of producers of 85 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) under the E-Waste (Management) Rules

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a clarificatory letter dated 9 May 2023 to the Customs authorities, extending interim arrangements for releasing imported consignments of producers of 85 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. Producers may continue to clear consignments on the basis of an undertaking until 30 June 2023, after which EPR Registration Certificates from the CPCB EPR Portal will be mandatory. Producers of 21 additional EEE items whose old EPR Authorisation has expired also get a parallel reprieve until the same date.
sections.what_changed: When the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 came into force on 1 April 2023, they replaced the 2016 Rules and introduced a new EPR Portal-based registration regime for producers, manufacturers, refurbishers and recyclers. The transition created a real-world bottleneck: imported consignments of EEE products were being held up at ports because many producers had not yet received their new EPR Registration Certificates. Through its 9 May 2023 letter to the Central Board of Indirect Taxes & Customs (CBIC), CPCB has clarified three things. First, the interim arrangement allowing release of consignments against an undertaking (originally communicated on 13 April 2023) stands extended until 30 June 2023. Second, the 30-day window earlier specified for submitting the EPR Registration Certificate has been replaced with a fixed cut-off date of 30 June 2023, counted from the date of undertaking submitted to Customs. Third, for 21 EEE categories (ITEW1–ITEW16 and CEEW1–CEEW5) whose EPR Authorisation under the 2016 Rules has expired, Customs may release consignments if producers submit an acknowledgement of fresh application on the EPR Portal along with the lapsed EPRA, again valid till 30 June 2023.
who_is_affected: Producers and importers of 85 newly notified EEE categories under the 2022 Rules — covering IT and Telecom Equipment (ITEW17–ITEW27), Consumer Electricals and Electronics (CEEW6–CEEW19), Large and Small Electrical Equipment (LSEEW1–LSEEW34), Electrical and Electronic Tools (EETW1–EETW8), Toys, Leisure and Sports Equipment (TLSEW1–TLSEW6), Medical Devices (MDW1–MDW10) and Laboratory Instruments (LIW1–LIW2)
Producers of 21 legacy EEE categories (ITEW1–ITEW16 and CEEW1–CEEW5) whose EPR Authorisation under the 2016 Rules has expired
Customs House Agents (CHAs), freight forwarders and logistics partners handling EEE shipments at Indian ports
Consumer electronics, IT hardware, medical device, laboratory equipment and small/large appliance brands importing finished or semi-finished EEE
Compliance, EHS, supply chain and trade-finance teams responsible for clearing imports
key_obligations: Until 30 June 2023: Producers may secure release of imported EEE consignments by submitting an undertaking to Customs along with the Bill of Entry
From 1 July 2023 onwards: Release of consignments will require a valid EPR Registration Certificate generated from the CPCB EPR Portal — no further extensions are indicated

Producers with expired EPRA (under 2016 Rules) for 21 EEE items: must submit a copy of the expired authorisation plus an acknowledgement of fresh application on the CPCB EPR Portal — also valid only till 30 June 2023
CPCB is maintaining a record of all undertakings submitted; producers should expect compliance verification post-deadline
CBIC has been asked to nominate a single point of contact to share compliance status with CPCB
sections.climate_angle: The 2022 E-Waste Rules represent India's most consequential shift toward a circular economy for electronics, introducing a digital, certificate-traded EPR framework comparable to producer responsibility regimes in the EU under the WEEE Directive and emerging schemes in Southeast Asia. For Indian operations of global companies, e-waste compliance is increasingly material under ISSB IFRS S2 (climate-related disclosures around resource use and transition risk) and CSRD/ESRS E5 (Resource Use and Circular Economy). Failure to clear imports or maintain EPR registration is no longer a routine customs hiccup — it directly affects working capital, demurrage costs, and Scope 3 inventory disclosures. Supply chain leaders should treat EPR registration with the same rigour as BIS certification or BRSR data assurance: as a structural license-to-operate issue tied to India's broader low-carbon, circular-economy transition.
what_to_do: Confirm immediately whether your products fall under any of the 85 new EEE categories or the 21 legacy categories — map your SKUs to the CPCB code list
If not already done, apply on the CPCB EPR Portal (eprewastecpcb.in) for registration as a Producer; track application status weekly
For shipments already at port, file the prescribed undertaking with Customs along with the Bill of Entry to avoid demurrage between now and 30 June 2023
Maintain a central register of all undertakings submitted, with linkage to Bill of Entry numbers, to support CPCB record-keeping and audit trails

Brief logistics partners, CHAs and treasury teams that interim relief expires on 30 June 2023 — plan import schedules accordingly and budget for potential port holding costs if registration is delayed
sections.our_take: This letter is a pragmatic but firm signal from CPCB: the regulator is willing to absorb short-term transition pain, but is closing the door on open-ended relief. The 30 June 2023 cut-off is unusually specific and replaces the rolling 30-day window — a deliberate move to force producers off interim undertakings and onto the formal EPR Portal. We expect CPCB to begin reconciling undertakings against registrations from Q3 FY24, and producers who continue to import without certification beyond June face the dual risk of consignment seizure and reputational exposure under the new EPR-credit trading mechanism. Boards and CFOs should now treat EPR compliance as a board-level supply chain risk, not a back-office filing — particularly with BRSR Core assurance, ISSB-aligned disclosures, and customer-led Scope 3 inquiries all converging in FY24–25.

posts.title: CPCB Greenlights Customs Release for 85 EEE Item Importers: Undertaking-Based Clearance Under E-Waste Rules 2022
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has written to the Central Board of Excise and Customs allowing the release of imported consignments of 85 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) covered under the new E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, subject to an undertaking from the Pro

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has written to the Central Board of Excise and Customs allowing the release of imported consignments of 85 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) covered under the new E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, subject to an undertaking from the Producer/Importer. This is a transitional relief because the EPR Portal — which Producers must register on — was still under upgradation and expected to go live by 30th April 2023. Importers must submit their EPR Registration Certificate within one month of giving the undertaking.
sections.what_changed: Effective 1st April 2023, the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 require every Producer of EEE — including Importers — to register with CPCB on the EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) Portal before placing products on the Indian market. Because the EPR Portal was not yet operational on the rules' effective date, importers of 85 specified EEE categories faced the risk of consignments being held at ports. CPCB has therefore directed Customs to release such consignments against a signed undertaking from the Producer, with the condition that the actual online EPR Registration Certificate is produced within 30 days. A copy of the undertaking must also be emailed to CPCB at ewaste2.cpcb@gov.in from the company's authorised email ID.
who_is_affected: Importers and domestic Producers of any of the 85 notified EEE items under the 2022 Rules
IT & Telecom Equipment manufacturers/importers (ITEW 17 to ITEW 27) — laptops, servers, routers, mobile devices and related equipment
Consumer Electricals & Electronics (CEEW 6 to CEEW 19) — televisions, refrigerators, washing machines, air conditioners and similar appliances
Large and Small Electrical & Electronic Equipment (LSEEW 1 to LSEEW 34) — a wide range of household and commercial appliances
Electrical & Electronic Tools (EETW 1 to EETW 8), Toys, Leisure & Sports Equipment (TLSEW 1 to TLSEW 6)
Medical Devices (MDW 1 to MDW 10) and Laboratory Instruments (LIW 1 to LIW 2)
Customs Houses, Customs Brokers (CHAs) and logistics partners handling these consignments
key_obligations: E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 came into force from 1st April 2023, replacing the 2016 Rules
EPR Portal expected to be operational by 30th April 2023 — Producers must register thereafter
Imported consignments will be released by Customs only against a signed undertaking on Producer company letterhead (format prescribed by CPCB)
A copy of the undertaking must be emailed to ewaste2.cpcb@gov.in from the company's authorised email ID
EPR Registration Certificate must be obtained and submitted to Customs within 1 month of giving the undertaking
Failure to comply may attract penalties under the Environment (Protection) Act and impact future import clearances
sections.climate_angle: The 2022 E-Waste Rules mark India's decisive shift to a fully digital, certificate-based EPR regime aligned with global circular economy norms. For multinationals reporting under ISSB (IFRS S2), CSRD or CDP, EPR registration data feeds directly into Scope 3 downstream emissions, product end-of-life disclosures and circularity KPIs. Mismanaged e-waste is also a material physical and reputational risk: hazardous leachates, informal sector burning and lost critical minerals (cobalt, lithium, rare earths) undermine both India's net-zero commitments and corporate supply chain resilience. Companies that treat EPR as a paperwork exercise will struggle when European importers, under CSRD and the upcoming Digital Product Passport, demand verifiable take-back and recycling data from their Indian supply chains.
what_to_do: Map every SKU your company imports or manufactures against the 85 EEE codes (ITEW, CEEW, LSEEW, EETW, TLSEW, MDW, LIW) to confirm applicability
Issue the prescribed undertaking on company letterhead for any in-transit or pending consignments and email a copy to ewaste2.cpcb@gov.in from an authorised ID before approaching Customs
Initiate EPR registration on the CPCB portal as soon as it goes live (30th April 2023) — assign a single owner internally and gather KYC, product specs, and projected sales/import volumes in advance
Engage a CPCB-registered PRO or recycler early and lock in EPR target compliance for FY 2023-24, including back-calculations under the new collection targets
Brief Customs Brokers and logistics partners on the undertaking-based release process to avoid demurrage; set up an internal tracker to ensure the EPR Certificate is filed with Customs within 30 days
sections.our_take: This CPCB letter is a pragmatic, one-time bridge — not a relaxation. The 30-day clock starts ticking the moment an importer signs the undertaking, and CPCB will almost certainly cross-check undertakings against final EPR registrations once the portal stabilises. We expect tighter enforcement, denial of future clearances for defaulters, and progressively higher EPR collection targets year on year. Companies that move now to consolidate their product master, EPR data architecture and PRO relationships will not only avoid port delays but also be ready for the next wave — a Digital Product Passport-style traceability regime that India is likely to converge towards by 2026.

posts.title: Plastic Waste Management (2nd Amendment) Rules 2022: EPR Tightens for Producers, Importers & Brand Owners
posts.summary: On 6 July 2022, the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Plastic Waste Management (Second Amendment) Rules, 2022, significantly strengthening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for plastic packaging. The amendment introduces clear definitions for

sections.summary: On 6 July 2022, the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Plastic Waste Management (Second Amendment) Rules, 2022, significantly strengthening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for plastic packaging.
